perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE VII - BILLINGS AND PAYMENTS Transporter shall bill and shipper shall pay all rates and charges in accordance with Articles V and VI, respectively, of the General Terms and Conditions of the FERC Gas Tariff.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE VII - BILLINGS AND PAYMENTS Transporter shall bill and shipper shall pay all rates and charges as Transporter deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit reference to the General Terms and Conditions of the FERC Gas Tariff regarding billing and payment, granting the transporter unilateral control over billing, which may conflict with regulations requiring transparent and justifiable billing practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 18 CFR § 154.309, which mandates that rates charged by natural gas companies be just and reasonable. By removing the reference to FERC Gas Tariff, the agreement could be interpreted to allow rates that are not just and reasonable, violating federal regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the explicit tie to the FERC Gas Tariff, which dictates 'just and reasonable' rates as per 18 CFR § 154.309. By allowing the transporter to bill as they 'deem appropriate,' the changed text opens the door to rates that may not be just and reasonable, contradicting the implicit requirement of the original agreement and potentially violating federal regulations. The scraped snippet 1 confirms the existence of 18 CFR 154.309. The omission of the reference to the FERC Gas Tariff creates a legal loophole which violates the legal standard, which is why this is an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE XI - WARRANTIES 11.1 In addition to the warranties set forth in Article IX of the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Shipper warrants the following: (a) Shipper warrants that all upstream and downstream transportation arrangements are in place, or will be in place as of the requested effective date of service, and that it has advised the upstream and downstream transporters of the receipt and delivery points under this Agreement and any quantity limitations for each point as specified on Exhibit "A" attached hereto. Shipper agrees to indemnify and hold Transporter harmless for refusal to transport gas hereunder in the event any upstream or downstream transporter fails to receive or deliver gas as contemplated by this Agreement. (b) Shipper agrees to indemnify and hold Transporter harmless from all suits, actions, debts, accounts, damages, costs, losses and expenses (including reasonable attorneys fees) arising from or out of breach of any warranty by Shipper herein.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE XI - WARRANTIES 11.1 Shipper warrants the following: (a) Shipper warrants that all upstream and downstream transportation arrangements are in place, or will be in place as of the requested effective date of service, and that it has advised the upstream and downstream transporters of the receipt and delivery points under this Agreement and any quantity limitations for each point as specified on Exhibit "A" attached hereto. Shipper agrees to indemnify and hold Transporter harmless for refusal to transport gas hereunder in the event any upstream or downstream transporter fails to receive or deliver gas as contemplated by this Agreement. (b) Shipper agrees to indemnify and hold Transporter harmless from all suits, actions, debts, accounts, damages, costs, losses and expenses (including reasonable attorneys fees) arising from or out of breach of any warranty by Shipper herein.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'In addition to the warranties set forth in Article IX of the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff,' the contract potentially eliminates certain consumer protections and standard warranties that are legally required, creating an imbalance of power.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification removes the existing protection of the consumer rights according to warranties set by Article IX of the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff. This is contradictory to 15 U.S. Code § 2308, which prohibits disclaimers of implied warranties under the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. By removing the reference to FERC Gas Tariff warranties, it could be argued that the contract attempts to disclaim or limit implied warranties, violating federal law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial action with respect to such product in the event that such
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the phrase 'In addition to the warranties set forth in Article IX of the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff.' The Law Explanation states that this removal potentially eliminates consumer protections and standard warranties that are legally required under 15 U.S. Code § 2308, which prohibits disclaimers of implied warranties. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50, concerns CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES, suggesting that the original contract provided coverage under consumer protection laws, and the removal of that reference could be seen as an attempt to disclaim or limit those warranties.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE XIII - NOTICE Except as otherwise provided in the General Terms and Conditions applicable to this Agreement, any notice under this Agreement shall be in writing and mailed to the post office address of the Party intended to receive the same, as follows: TRANSPORTER: Tennessee Gas Pipeline Company P. O. Box 2511 Houston, Texas 77252-2511 Attention: Director, Transportation Control SHIPPER: NOTICES: Louisville Gas and Electric Company P. O. Box 32020 Louisville, Kentucky 40232 Attention: J. Clay Murphy, Dir - Gas Management, Planning and Supply BILLING: Louisville Gas and Electric Company P. O. Box 32020 Louisville, Kentucky 40232 Attention: J. Clay Murphy, Dir - Gas Management, Planning and Supply or such other address as either Party shall designate by formal written notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE XIII - NOTICE Any notice under this Agreement shall be provided at the discretion of the involved party.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the structured notification protocol, eliminating the requirement for written notice via mail and giving the power to the transporter to communicate or not in a way that works for them.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the structured notification protocol and replacing it with 'at the discretion of the involved party,' the contract contradicts Kentucky Revised Statute § 446.040. This statute requires contracts to be interpreted in a way that assumes good faith and fair dealing. Eliminating formal notice requirements undermines the ability of parties to effectively monitor and enforce the contract, thereby hindering good faith and fair dealing.

Previous Next Download as PDF 446.040 Adjournment of proceedings over weekend. If the law authorizes a court, or the proceedings of any tribunal or officer, to be adjourned from day to day, an adjournment from Saturday until Monday shall be legal. Effective: October 1, 1942 History: Recodified 1942 Ky. Acts ch. 208, sec. 1, effective October 1, 1942, from Ky. Stat. sec. 455. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a detailed notification protocol, while the changed text replaces it with discretionary notification. The Law Explanation states this contradicts Kentucky Revised Statute § 446.040 because it undermines the ability of parties to effectively monitor and enforce the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2 shows the statute exists, although it pertains to adjournment of proceedings over a weekend, suggesting that the LLM Law Explanation is not directly supported. However, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that the original text's notification protocol was essential, and its omission creates a legal loophole making the contract non-compliant. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' where a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted.
